text: The surgeon carefully manipulates the stomach, which is herniated into the mediastinum, attempting to reduce the herniated contents back into the abdominal cavity.
The distal body and pylorus of the stomach are visible, having herniated into the mediastinum.
The surgeon examines the interior of a large hiatal hernia sac, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the hernia repair by creating a dissection plane between the hernia sac and the mediastinum, carefully separating the sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon incises the junction of the hernia sac with the right crus of the diaphragm, aiming to separate the sac from the surrounding diaphragm tissue.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to carefully enter the mediastinum from the exterior of the hernia sac, progressing with gentle separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the margins of the hiatus, aiming to access the avascular plane between the hernia sac and the mediastinum.

The surgeon performs extensive blunt dissection to separate the hernia sac from the mediastinum, addressing adhesions due to the patient's long-standing hernia. The video shows both the inside and outside of the sac.

The surgeon completes dissection from six o'clock to twelve o'clock on the right side and begins to reach the apex of the hernia sac. The dissection progresses deep into the mediastinum, making it challenging for the camera to capture the apex of the sac.

After reducing the entire hernia sac, the anterior vagus nerve, left vagus nerve, and esophagus are visible. The surgeon proceeds to dissect posterior to the esophagus to fully mobilize the gastroesophageal junction.

Umbilical tape is tied around the gastroesophageal (GE) junction, showing a good length of the esophagus positioned within the abdomen. The surgeon checks for any retraction of the GE junction back into the thorax, confirming that it remains stable within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon prepares the stomach for fundoplication by dividing the greater omentum from the margins of the greater curvature of the stomach. Short gastric vessels are also divided during this process.
The surgeon uses a harmonic or ligature device to assist in the dissection, revealing the right vagus nerve lying on the posterior surface of the esophagus near the gastroesophageal junction.
Following the fundic mobilization, the surgeon performs the shoe shine maneuver to ensure the wrap is not twisted, adjusting the position of the stomach fundus around the esophagus.
The surgeon closes the hiatal defect using interrupted non-absorbable Ethibond sutures, preparing the area for the upcoming fundoplication.

The surgeon ensures the knot at the hiatus is not too tight to prevent muscle atrophy. Following this, the fundoplication procedure is performed, wrapping the stomach around the esophagus to prevent reflux.

The surgeon performs a 360-degree Nissen's fundoplication, wrapping the stomach around the esophagus in a manner that allows a 5mm instrument to pass between the wrap and the esophagus, ensuring the wrap remains 'floppy'.
The surgeon places two sutures two centimeters apart to form the wrap, ensuring it covers the distal part of the intra-abdominal esophagus with a total thickness of two centimeters.

The surgeon fixes the completed wrap to the gastroesophageal (GE) junction, ensuring it is secure to prevent migration. The tension-free lower part of the esophagus is visible, indicating successful completion of the wrap.
The wrap is visibly covering the lower two centimeters of the esophagus. The surgeon completes the repair of the hiatus using a composite mesh.
